clinical_history: ECG from a patient status post a coronary angioplasty procedure shows which ONE of the following findings? (Note: lead V5 was not recorded.)
diagnosis: This anomalous appearing ECG shows baseline artifact (simulating atrial fibrillation) with underlying sinus rhythm. Inadvertent reversal of the left and right arm electrodes accounts for the "strange" P wave and QRS axes, associated with a negative P and negative QRS combination in lead I (in concert with a positive P and positive QRS in lead aVR). The QRS findings mimic those of a high lateral Q wave MI.  A corrected limb lead ECG can be imagined  if you do the following: "flip" lead I, reverse leads II and III, and reverse leads aVR and aVL. After these corrective maneuvers, note  that the true QRS axis is close to 0 degrees and, therefore, does not qualify for left axis deviation (-30 degrees or more negative), and certainly not for left anterior fascicular block (-45 degree or more negative).
